title: The Energy Shock Is Testing Government Budgets
gist: The IMF's new Global Policy Tracker finds governments responding to the 2026 Middle East war energy shock with mostly untargeted, costly price-containment measures that resemble the 2022 playbook, but amid tighter fiscal space and rising debt service these choices risk becoming permanent, masking contingent liabilities, and collectively amplifying the shock.

themes.theme: Scale and composition of the policy response

insights.claim: The IMF's new Global Policy Tracker has recorded nearly 900 policy measures across about 170 countries since the 2026 Middle East war began, with fiscal measures dominating the response to higher energy prices.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: Signals broad, coordinated price suppression that can keep global energy demand inelastic and prices elevated longer than markets expect.
insights.quote: A new IMF Global Policy Tracker has recorded nearly 900 policy measures introduced across about 170 countries since the beginning of the war
insights.locator: A new IMF Global Policy Tracker has recorded

insights.claim: In advanced economies, almost half of measures responding to the 2026 energy shock are subsidies to energy producers and distributors and roughly another third are cuts to fuel excise taxes.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: Excise cuts and producer subsidies are direct fiscal drains that signal widening deficits in DM bond markets.
insights.quote: in advanced economies, almost half of the measures are subsidies to energy producers and distributors. Another third are cuts to fuel excise taxes

insights.claim: Emerging economies have used a more varied mix including fuel price caps and pricing-formula adjustments, while high-debt countries lean on price controls and demand suppression such as fuel rationing, mandated remote work, and travel restrictions.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: Demand rationing in EMs is a tell that fiscal space is exhausted; watch for activity drag in those economies.
insights.quote: have relied more on pricing measures and demand suppression, including through fuel rationing, mandated remote work, and travel restrictions

insights.claim: Many energy-support measures described as temporary lack clear expiration dates or fiscal cost estimates, creating a pathway for interim support to become permanent and increasingly costly if prices stay elevated.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: Sticky subsidies imply structurally wider deficits even after the shock fades, a credit-negative for exposed sovereigns.
insights.quote: a large share of measures described as temporary lack clear expiration dates or fiscal cost estimates. This is how interim support can become permanent
insights.locator: Noble, but potentially costly and risky intentions

insights.claim: Pricing measures that compress margins, especially at state-owned energy companies, generate losses that later surface as contingent liabilities on the public balance sheet rather than appearing in standard fiscal accounts.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: Off-balance-sheet SOE losses are an underpriced sovereign risk; scrutinize national oil/utility company balance sheets.
insights.quote: Pricing measures that compress margins—especially in state-owned energy companies—can generate losses that later surface as contingent liabilities on the public balance sheet.

insights.claim: When many countries simultaneously shield consumers from energy prices, demand responds less, contributing to tighter markets and potentially higher global prices, so individually rational policies can collectively amplify the shock.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: Global price suppression is bullish for oil prices via suppressed demand elasticity; a contrarian read on subsidy-heavy environments.
insights.quote: widespread suppression of price pass-through can weaken adjustment at the global level... Individually rational policies can collectively amplify the shock.

insights.claim: Debt service burdens are rising and fiscal space remains limited for many countries, making policy design in the 2026 shock more consequential than in the comparable 2022 energy shock.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: The macro buffer is thinner than in 2022, so a prolonged shock has higher tail risk for sovereign stress.
insights.quote: debt service burdens are rising for many countries and fiscal space remains limited, amid an environment of heightened uncertainty and recurrent shocks
insights.locator: The tracker illustrates an important pattern

insights.claim: The IMF advises governments to protect people not prices by allowing energy prices to adjust while keeping fiscal interventions temporary and targeted, preserving fiscal space for future shocks.
insights.speaker: Era Dabla-Norris
insights.so_what: Countries that follow this advice will look more fiscally resilient; subsidy-heavy peers face escalating costs if conflict re-escalates.
insights.quote: The principle remains simple: protect people, not prices.
